sentences.text: I'm trying to figure out.
I left Ungwar.
The Germans came in in April, the beginning of April-- let's assume the 7th of April.
I was in the ghetto three weeks to the end of April.
And I left Ungwar, the ghetto, the end of April.
I was in-- arrived in Auschwitz six days later.
And, and I stayed in Auschwitz, yeah.
So I left Auschwitz in the middle of May, and I arrived in Warsaw in the middle of May of 1944.
And I stayed in, in, in Warsaw 10 weeks-- June, July-- to the end of July, 1944.
Now life in the Warsaw ghetto-- the Warsaw ghetto had many kommandos, but everyone was working on bricks-- not really.
Some of them were working on metal, too.
This is destroyed buildings.
They-- we restored material fit for construction and shipped to Germany.
Now, there were some people-- and we had the quotas.
Everybody was assigned and in a day, you have to do so many and so many.
And they counted it, too.
So I-- as far as my notes say, I was working on bricks most of the time.
Some days, we were OK.
And some days, the sun was terrible.
We still had to produce the same amount.
And some days, it was pouring all day, and their orders were we have to produce the same amount.
Those are the days that most of us wished we were dead instead of suffering.
All right.
In the ghetto, if the people finished the work-- let's assume I had to produce an amount of 300 bricks a day, and I finished at 3 o'clock instead of six, I could walk around because I did my quota.
Nobody-- the, the, the people-- the supervisors were civilians from German companies, and they really didn't care what we were doing as long as we did the quota.
And a little by little, we got acquainted with civilian workers, Polish civilian workers, and we made connections, according to my notes.
And most of us worked a little harder, gave an extra 100 bricks.
They loaded their own wagons or whatever for bread.
I did that.
So according to my notes, in Warsaw, I really didn't go hungry because I had some extra rations.
I did a little extra work and we-- and apparently the supervisors, the German supervisors, didn't care as long as they had their quota.
They knew about it.
Not these SS officers-- if the SS officer found out, there would be beatings.
No, but the German-- the German supervisors, construction supervisors that were sent from Germany to manage this, they didn't really care.
So most of the people that were in Warsaw had, had what to eat.
And the washroom was clean, and the living quarters were bearable.
And Sundays, we were off.
But Sundays we had to wash our clothes-- by hand, of course-- and otherwise, otherwise we'd be infected with lice and who knows what.
Sundays.
But the Germans didn't let us-- let us-- let us rest Sunday.
They told us to do menial work, take stuff, take soil from here.
Put it there.
And then take it back-- just to keep busy and do something.
Resting was not in their book apparently.
So this lasted till, as I said-- what did I say-- June and July.
We cleaned.
We cleaned our own barracks.
We cleaned it on Sundays.
And the kapos, the, the kapos, the in charge of the barracks, they made sure that the barracks are clean.
And the washrooms, we were assigned-- every barracks had a few people.
Every second they cleaned the washrooms, and they were really clean.
And we had a little soap, so compared with Auschwitz, this was like heaven.
And they also had the-- an infirmary where they were-- oh, we had our own doctors.
There were so many doctors among us.
So some of our own people were doctors in the infirmary.
So I don't know how much medication they had.
They had some.
So if somebody really needed something, so our own doctors did their best to save us.
And they told us what to eat, what not to eat, what water to drink and so it was survivable at Warsaw.
At the end of-- in the end of July, we already heard-- and, and once in a while, they smuggled in some newspapers so we could read what's going on, into the camp.
The Polish civilian workers brought some newspapers, and we paid for it.
We had some Polish money.
Sometimes the Polish people, the Polish civilian workers paid for bricks with money, Polish money.
And Polish money could get something.
For Polish money, you could buy bread.
Yeah, they brought in bread, a loaf of bread.
They can get almost everything.
I did that, yes.
And then most of the other people did that.
And they-- and we read that the Russians are approaching this area already.
And we were very upbeat reading these reports.
And one day, we already heard some, some artillery.
So they couldn't be too far if we heard it.
So we figured we're not going to be here forever now.
There's two choices for the Germans-- eliminate the whole camp with machine guns, or evacuate the camp.
So we didn't know which course they're going to take.
So as far as I remember, one day, they told us to pack whatever you have-- which we didn't have much of.
Everybody had a plate, a knife, a spoon.
That's all we owned, I figure.
And that we will be-- and they gave us extra bread, and we were marching for days.
And from there, we didn't have-- and they explained, and this is amazing.
They explained to us we wouldn't march if we had transportation, but transportation like trucks is used by the army.
They didn't assign enough transportation to us, so we have to march to a railroad station.
They themselves were not happy to march so many miles, the German guards.
Now, the said it on the way, anybody who cannot keep up with the march will be left behind.
They said it in advance, and they said try to keep up the tempo because we have a deadline to meet because they didn't spell it out that the Russians are behind us, but we figured it out.
They just said we have a deadline to meet.
We have to be at a cert-- that certain a railroad track or station on a certain day, at a certain time.
About three days we marched and unfortunately we lost some of our people because they couldn't keep up.
It was hot.
It was July or August.
It was very, very hot.
And we didn't have water.
We had a little water.
Wherever we could stop, we-- they let us go into a lake to get a  little water.
They figured if we don't get it, we'll all die on the way.
That's all.
So they had to deliver some, some producing people to their superiors in Germany, I figured.
We, in that group, I have no idea, but I would estimate 2,000 to 3,000-- about 3,000.
So figure 50 in a car, so 60 cars.
Can a railroad take 60 cars?
That's too long right?
We were walking to a railroad siding.
About 3,000 of us.
And we count a few-- a few that didn't make it because of the heat.
And also, they didn't make it because when we went in for  water into a lake, they didn't give us enough time.
So those people that couldn't, they drank and drank.
They couldn't get back to the group on time.
They left them there.
That's it.
Not that I know of, no.
They left them there.
Whether they survived or not is a big question mark, not because of the German population was so hateful.
The German population, if they saw a haeftling, a prisoner, were reporting him for one reason.
They didn't want to be involved with the Gestapo.
The Gestapo was ruthless with their own people, too.
That's how there were so many German prisoners in Auschwitz.
They committed something-- a crime, whatever, or against the regime.
They said something against Hitler.
So we lost a few people.
I don't know the exact number.
And then, we got on the trains, and we had enough food.
And the train, they didn't-- I mean they may have told us where we are going to another camp in Germany named Dachau.
We never heard of Dachau, but they told us we're going, and how long it will approximately take.
It took days, days.
Now at night, the train stopped at night.
I don't know why they, they stopped, but some of our people opened the door somehow, and they sneaked out.
Whether they survived or not, I have no idea.
No.
But in the morning, the, the SS guards brought in dogs.
They searched the whole area.
Didn't find any.
They didn't find any.
They disappeared into the fields or whatever, and they didn't find any.
As far as I remember, from our group, about 10 people escaped.
They didn't punish us.
They just mentioned it over and over.
If anybody-- if we catch anybody escaping, the penalty is death-- no beating, nothing, just death.
That's it.
And, and they couldn't find them.
They, they looked and looked.
So after three days, we arrived in Dachau.
And Dachau was a big camp, an extermination camp-- which we didn't know at that time when we entered-- but very clean.
Compared with Auschwitz, it's very clean and neat and organized.
And as far as my notes are concerned, I don't remember everything I read.
It was a better condition than, than Auschwitz.
Better conditions for the prisoners.
They had better conditions for prisoners than Auschwitz.
In Dachau, they gave me a number.
So I found the number in my notebook.
You have the number, right?
Yes.
It was a short number.
It was sewn onto the prison, prison uniform.
And we stayed in Dachau.
We were assigned a big barracks the way we had them in Auschwitz.
But as far as I remember, it was cleaner, and they may have given us a little more food then in Auschwitz.
In Dachau, the same.
And the barracks had the same amount of people-- 800 in a barracks.
But somehow, as far as I know, Dachau was much cleaner, better organized, a little more food.
And the treatment was better, as far as I know.
I only was with people that I knew from the camp, all along-- yes, a number of people, a number of people my age.
And yes, a number of people I knew.
And we stayed there about seven days, seven days in Dachau.
And then--
We didn't do much.
They had appel, same as in Auschwitz, same system, and then a little soup.
And then, we could stay in the sun and talk and this and that.
About the war.
Hopefully, it will be over soon.
So we go home or whatever.
We'll see our parents or relatives.
That's about-- that's the main topic.
Some people are pessimistic, some people are optimistic.
Yeah, the war's going to be ended before, and we'll be able to survive.
I know I didn't say what I thought, I thought.
But I made myself believe that if I went through hell so far, that I'll make it.

I made-- I made believe that I tried to make it because half of the-- to go through hell and work in Warsaw on a rainy day and produce 300 bricks or in a 90 degree day, that is-- that's very bad and so on.
Even the first train to, to Auschwitz, I mean the trip to Auschwitz was terrible.
So many people didn't have the will, so they die.
That's all.
They give up.
They gave up, and that's it.
They die.
So I hoped I'd be able to keep somehow.
I didn't know.
I wasn't sure, but I was one of the optimists.
And after seven days of hanging around in Dachau, we were-- we were rounded up sort of.
And we were put into a train, as far as I know.
It may have been a civilian train, a passenger train for a change, which I, I couldn't-- I couldn't believe.
But it was a short trip, maybe three, four hours, to a station named Kaufering.
Kaufering, that's not Landsberg.
But Kaufering is a station in the vicinity of Landsberg.
Landsberg didn't have a railroad station, but the whole area was Landsberg.
At Kaufering was a railroad station.
We got out and groups were formed.
And they were recounted, smaller groups this time.
Landsberg had seven camps and each camp got a few people, like 200.
I'm talking not thousands-- these are small camps-- hundreds.
So as far as I know, a group of 200 was formed, and I was in one of those 200.
And we were marched from Kaufering to a certain camp.
The camps at that time-- the, the structure of the camps was not finished.
Prisoners that came before us worked on the camp, building the camp-- building this, building that.
But the camp consisted of a-- of a tunnel underground.
That's it.
A tunnel sort of underground.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's about it-- with bunks and maybe wooden walls around it.
And it was terrible.
I mean the structure was not-- it was built by amateurs, by people who were students at home, who knows-- with supervision, of course.
And as far as I know, this was the camp and so many people are assigned to each camp.
And there were prisoners who were there a few weeks earlier, and they were the old timers already.
There was no real old timers, like three years.
The whole-- whole camp wasn't old.
And they explained that after it's all finished, we'll all be assigned work.
And it's not going to be easy.
Because from 6:00 to 6:00-- from 6:00 AM to 6:00 PM is 12 hours.
Of the 12 hours, you march four, two and two-- whether it's hot or cold-- two hours one way to perform work.
And this was all in Germany already.
So we hoped.
We hoped after a day or two.
And the appel was the same way as in Auschwitz.
You got to get up at 4 o'clock in the morning and stand outside, rain or snow or shine.
And since it was September already, it was cool already.
Over there, we'd stand outside in the morning.
Some people got sick just standing there because their clothing wasn't enough.
Well, whoever couldn't, got sick.
Got sick.
They stayed behind, that's all.
Stayed behind-- those that stayed behind, some of them went to the hospital, what they called a sort of infirmary.
And some of them stayed in the barracks.
And it was not-- it was not a very good deal to stay in.
It was easier than do work, or much easier.
They only-- had only one catch-- Germans once in a while came with trucks and picked up these sick ones and took them away.
You never heard of them since.
I managed, somehow.
So here is the catch again.
Some kommandos-- these are workplaces-- offered a little food, somehow.
It wasn't legal, of course.
Now as far as my notes say, I was working on the railroad tracks, and those tracks that were bombed by the Allies.
We had to restore them and rebuild them, but there was no food there, nothing.
I was working on the tracks for about a month or possibly six weeks.
So I tried to get out of there and get to another kommando.
Sometimes, we succeeded.
We succeeded.
We ran around early in the morning from one group to another.
The Germans only counted heads.
They didn't-- they didn't read numbers, not in the morning.
They need 90 in this group, 90.
So whoever I could catch or if I had somebody in another group and he said my group is great.
You can get a little break.
You sneaked in, that's all.
Who did you go with?
You had friends that you did this, or all by yourself?
Yeah, I usually, I had friends.
They came with me.
They find out that-- like I worked in a-- in loading railroad cars or unloading actually for the SS, for the-- for the army, for the German army.
So while I was unloading the bread, I put a half a loaf-- a big loaf would be too much.
So I put a half a loaf in there into-- hidden on my body.
Of course, if they-- if they caught me, there would have been torture.
But most of them, most of us, they didn't catch us.
And the Germans didn't keep exactly 100% a record. 100 or 500 or so, they had 480 loaves.
I mean we didn't-- we couldn't take many, and the same with butter and so on.
So it helped and every little bit helped.
And so I stayed on in that kommando for about six weeks, I figure.
And then, I was assigned to another kommando, construction work.
And that was very bad because I had to do work at night.
They had night shifts too to do construction.
It was not civilian construction.
It was-- I believe, it was construction for the army, army buildings.
But there was no food, no extra food.
And it was hard work, and it was no good.
So I, I kept switching kommandos.
And then, one day, I was assigned to a kommando that was digging ditches somewhere in fields, but there was food in the fields.
It was turnips.
And we could-- actually, we could eat while we were working-- raw turnips, but it's life sustaining.
And of course, we couldn't do it while the SS guards.
Once in a while, you had SS guards, they didn't care.
They didn't care.
They turned the other way, but that was rare.
Usually, they'd beat us if they found it.
They didn't shoot us for that.
You take a turnip form the field, you ate it, it's OK.
And in the same area we were digging ditches-- I don't remember why these ditches or what-- or what the purpose of the ditches were.
I don't remember.
And there was groups of girls walking by.
And these girls worked in the offices or whatever.
And they dropped bread into the ditches.
On the way, they dropped bread in the ditches as they walked by.
So that helped.
And this was going on for a while and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until November, I believe, or so.
And then it got very cold and once in a while, I stayed off as sick.
I stayed home as sick and hoped that they won't catch me.
It was It was.
It was dangerous because once in a while-- they didn't come every day with trucks.
Occasionally, they rounded up the, the sick and the disabled and so on.
Well, and I would have to consult my notes to get all the details, but I don't have it.
As far as I know, at the end of November or December, I couldn't go to work anymore.
I was so weak, and I didn't have enough food.
So I was just hanging around, live on what little rations they had.
I could hardly walk.
And the number of people dying in the camp was increasing every day.
And they had a group that were just hauling dead people away from the-- from the-- from the-- they emptied out the barracks and hauled away the dead people.
There was more dead than alive because in time, it took-- it took its toll.
You know, you couldn't go to work because your little bread you got every day, it wasn't enough.
It wasn't enough.
And the heavy work, they just couldn't, couldn't manage.
So I had no choice, whatever is going to happen is going to happen.
That's all.
So I stayed in for the rest of the time till, till the end of April.
Yeah.
And we hoped for the best.
That's all.
I saw many people in my barracks-- which was a small barracks-- dying daily, daily.
In the evening, they laid down and in the morning, just gone.
And some of us, did survive.
And then once in a while, they had the delousing.
They came in, and they said everybody out.
Take your clothes.
And they emptied the whole barracks because there was typhoid or something in the camp.
And they, themselves didn't want to die.
The guards, they could get it too.
So they cleaned out the whole camp.
All the clothing they put into machines to clean them.
And the people had to go to showers, regular showers once in while.
And the, the straw in the camps was thrown out, cleaned out.
Unfortunately, according to my notes, it didn't last long.
The barracks was clean for four days.
After four days, these lice came back.
And the lice ate up the people.
Some people, there were so many lice, that they, they virtually killed the people-- the lice.
Not only the weakness, the lice killed a lot of people.
So that was a situation, and I hung on.
And then, one day, one day I was surprised that they said-- I heard artillery again nearby.
So I figured it can't be German artillery.
They wouldn't shoot their own people in their own buildings, destroy their own buildings.
So we didn't know exactly if it was Americans or Russians or British.
We already knew from experience in Warsaw that the war cannot last too long.
So now we were faced-- the few of us that were in the barracks alive, we were faced with the same question.
What are you German SS going to do?
Are they going to eliminate us?
Are they going to let us go?
Are they going to march just someplace?
We didn't know the answer to that.
On--
Actually, the SS a day later came in, and they said, anybody who could walk get up, get dressed, and walk.
Anybody who cannot walk, stay here.
So I still could walk a little.
So I got my stuff together, and we formed groups and the SS guards on the side.
And we were marching.
Now how far we were marching, I don't know.
But we didn't march for days because as far-- I got shot in the last minute by an SS guard in the field right there.
I don't know why.
Just for fun, he shot people.
There's no, no, no sense.
He didn't kill people, but he shot them anyway.
He wounded them, that's all, as far as I know.
One of my legs got shot one day before liberation by an SS guard who went crazy shooting people.
So I got shot, and I couldn't march anymore because of the-- I was bleeding.
My foot was bleeding.
I was in a field and I was hiding behind a tree.
I figured whatever happens, happens.
So luckily, a day later, they came back with trucks-- SS and prisoners, helpers, strong prisoners and they picked up the people who were wounded.
Apparently, those SS guards wanted to make a name for themselves before the Allies came in or to show or prove that they save people instead of hurting people.
So we were taken to another camp.
And as far as I remember, and we were placed in a clean barracks and not so many people in a barracks.
I figured there weren't so many of us.
All right, Harry.
I'll stop you.
OK.
So one more tape and that's it?
